EDCS-03700780.summary: A short private letter or note, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-06100519.text_edition: \n Sarapi Caius Ramnius Cypronianus centurio legionis XXII Deiotarianae votum solvit\n
EDCS-06100519.translation: Gaius Ramnius Cypronianus, centurion of the XXII Deiotariana legion, paid his vow.
EDCS-06100519.summary: This is a votive inscription by a centurion of the XXII Deiotariana legion, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-06100520.text_edition: \n Marcus Terentius Longus miles legionis III Cuirenaicae sic centuria Arrecini vixit annos mens\n
EDCS-06100520.translation: Marcus Terentius Longus, a soldier of the III Cyrenaica legion, in the century of Arrecinus, lived for [X] months.
EDCS-06100520.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Terentius Longus, a soldier of the III Cyrenaica legion, who died after living for an incomplete number of months.

EDCS-07400700.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatorum Caesarum Marco Aurelio Antonino et Lucio Aurelio Vero Augustis dominis nostris principia a novo aedificatum sic est sic sub Volusium sic Maecianum sic praefectum Aegypti Aelium Agath\n
EDCS-07400700.translation: For the safety of our Lords Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augusti, the principia were rebuilt from new. This was done under Volusius Maecianus, prefect of Egypt, and Aelius Agath[---].
EDCS-07400700.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of the principia (headquarters building) during the co-reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus (161-169 CE) under the prefect Volusius Maecianus.

EDCS-07400701.text_edition: \n ustis Felix Priminia legio Constantiano (tres) (quattuor) Auggustorum\n
EDCS-07400701.summary: This is a fragmented inscription possibly related to a military unit, the Constantiana legion, and lists numbers or ranks. The approximate date is likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-07600460.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Servili<u=X>s Quinti filius Palatina Rufus Lepci Magna vixit annis XXVIII militavit annis IV Caius Iulius Rogatus heres eius Quintus Caecilius Martialis miles legionis III Cy<i>renaicae |(centuriae) Anti Vibiani vixit annis XXXV militavit annis XIII curam egit Gaius Maesius Demetrius legionis eiusdem |(centuriae) eiusdem heres eius hic situs est \n
EDCS-07600460.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Servilius Rufus, son of Quintus, of the Palatina tribe, from Lepci Magna, lived 28 years, served 4 years. Caius Iulius Rogatus, his heir, Quintus Caecilius Martialis, a soldier of the III Cyrenaica legion, of the century of Vibius, lived 35 years, served 13 years. The supervisor was Gaius Maesius Demetrius of the same legion and century. His heir is buried here.
EDCS-07600460.summary: This is a detailed funerary inscription for Quintus Servilius Rufus and Quintus Caecilius Martialis, both soldiers of the III Cyrenaica legion, dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08900643.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Valerius Omuncio eques Valerini filius fecit monimentum sic Valerini fili qui vixit menses VII\n
EDCS-08900643.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Valerius Omuncio, son of Valerius, an equestrian, made this monument for his son Valerius, who lived 7 months.
EDCS-08900643.summary: This funerary inscription records a father dedicating a monument for his infant son, Marcus Valerius Omuncio, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08900644.text_edition: \n Aurelia Iulia Epictesis origine Cilicissa quae vixit nnos XXXV item Marcus Aurelius Paullus filius e<i=L>us qui vixit nnos XII Aurelius Timocrates miles lecionis sic II Traianae fortis coniugi et filio \n
EDCS-08900644.translation: Aurelia Iulia Epictesis, originally from Cilicia, who lived 35 years. Likewise Marcus Aurelius Paullus, her son, who lived 12 years. Aurelius Timocrates, a soldier of the II Traiana Fortis legion, for his wife and son.
EDCS-08900644.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelius Timocrates, a soldier of the II Traiana Fortis legion, for his wife Aurelia Iulia Epictesis and son Marcus Aurelius Paullus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08900645.text_edition: \n Quintus Aebutius Quinti filius Quirina I eques in ala Apriana decurio alae eiusdem missus honesta missione vixit annosVIII ET EVM TOOMMONRIS VA CO\n
EDCS-08900645.translation: Quintus Aebutius, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, an equestrian, a decurion of the ala Apriana, was honourably discharged. He lived 8 years AND THEY [---]
EDCS-08900645.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription commemorating Quintus Aebutius, an equestrian decurion of the ala Apriana, who received an honourable discharge and lived for 8 years, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09401224.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni et Isidi Augustae pro salute et victoria Imperatorum nostrorum Antonini et Veri Augustorum sub Tito Flavio Titiano praefecto Aegypti et Attio Restituto praefecto kastrorum oboliscos sic duos Titus Aurelius Restitutus |(centurio) legionis II Traianae fortis curator cohortis I Flaviae Cilicum equitatae de suo posuit Servilio Pudente et Fuficio Pollione consulibus\n
EDCS-09401224.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, and Augusta Isis, for the welfare and victory of our Emperors Antoninus and Verus Augusti, Titus Aurelius Restitutus, centurion of Legio II Traiana Fortis, curator of the Cohors I Flaviae Cilicum equitatae, erected these two obelisks at his own expense, during the consulship of Servilius Pudens and Fuficius Pollio, under Titus Flavius Titianus, prefect of Egypt, and Attius Restitutus, prefect of the camp.
EDCS-09401224.summary: This inscription records the erection of two obelisks by a centurion for the welfare and victory of the emperors Antoninus and Verus, during their reign in Egypt.

EDCS-09700762.text_edition: \n pro salute Imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini et Publi Septimi Getae sub Claudio Iuliano praefecto Aegypti et Claudio Alexandro epistratego Aurelius Avitianus trierarchus posuit\n
EDCS-09700762.translation: For the welfare of the Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and Publius Septimius Geta, Aurelius Avitianus, trierarch, erected this, under Claudius Julianus, prefect of Egypt, and Claudius Alexander, epistrategus.
EDCS-09700762.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aurelius Avitianus, a trierarch, for the welfare of Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta in Egypt.

EDCS-11300895.summary: This is a lengthy list of soldiers, officers, and their origins, granting Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to veterans of Legio II Traiana Fortis and associated units serving in Egypt, during the reign of Marcus Aurelius and Commodus.

EDCS-11700082.text_edition: \n Dida Damanai filius nationi sic Volquorum sic eques alae Vocontiorum turma Maturi armatum sic feci stationi sic menses quinque pro salutem sic imperatore sic feliciter\n
EDCS-11700082.translation: Dida, son of Damana, of the Volqi tribe, a cavalryman in the turma of Maturus, belonging to the ala Vocontiorum, made this armed monument for five months of service during the emperor's happy reign.
EDCS-11700082.summary: This inscription records a five-month service by Dida, a cavalryman of the ala Vocontiorum, potentially in Egypt.

EDCS-11700252.text_edition: \n Caius Luconius eques cohortis I Flaviae Cilicum eques turmae Scaevae hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-11700252.translation: Caius Luconius, a cavalryman of Cohors I Flaviae Cilicum, of the turma Scaeva, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-11700252.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Caius Luconius, a cavalryman serving in Egypt.

EDCS-11700258.text_edition: \n Caius Numidius Eros hic fuit anno XXIIX Caesaris exs sic India rediens menos Phamenoth\n
EDCS-11700258.translation: Caius Numidius Eros was here in his 28th year, returning from India during the month of Phamenoth.
EDCS-11700258.summary: This inscription notes the presence of Caius Numidius Eros in Egypt, returning from India.

EDCS-11700261.text_edition: \n Primus Sextus Mevius Celeris centuria legionis XXII hic futuit\n
EDCS-11700261.translation: Primus, Sextus, and Mevius Celeris, centuria of Legio XXII, were here.
EDCS-11700261.summary: This inscription records the presence of three individuals, likely soldiers, from a centuria of Legio XXII in Egypt.

EDCS-11700262.text_edition: \n Anno IIII Lucius Iulius Ursus praefectus Aegypti hoc loco hydreuma ampliavit et cum esset insecurum praesidium fortius aedificari iussit curam agente operis Marco Trebonio Valente praefecto Berenicidis\n
EDCS-11700262.translation: In the 4th year, Lucius Julius Ursus, prefect of Egypt, enlarged the hydreuma in this place and, because it was insecure, ordered stronger defenses to be built, with Marcus Trebonius Valens, prefect of the Berenicis region, overseeing the work.
EDCS-11700262.summary: This inscription records the expansion and fortification of a water supply installation (hydreuma) in Egypt by the prefect Lucius Julius Ursus.

EDCS-12100050.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100050.text_edition: \n Imperator Ca<e=I>sar divi Antonini filius divi Veri Parthici Maximi frater divi Hadriani nepos divi Traiani Parthici pronepos divi Nervae abnepos Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXXIII imperator IX consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Antonini Augusti filius divi Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Germanicus Sarmaticus tribunicia potestate III sic imperator II consul II pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis quattuor quae appellantur veterana Gallica et Thracum Mauretanica et Vocontiorum et Apriana provincialis et cohortibus novem I Ulpia equitata Afrorum et I Augusta nnorum et I Apamenorum et I Lusitanorum et I Flavia equitata Cilicum et II Thracum et I sic Ituraeorum et III Itureorum et II Thebeorum et sunt in Aegypto sub Pactumeio Magno praefecto quinis et vicenis pluribusvsve stipendis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum is quas posttea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem X Kalendas Apriles Tito Flavio Claudiano Lucio Aemilio Iunco consulibus cohortis II Thracum cui praeest Caecilius Maior ex peditibus Plutioni Tithoeti filio Coptite descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Antonini filius divi Veri Parthici Maximi frater divi Hadriani nepos divi Traiani Parthici pronepos divi Nervae abnepos Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus pontifex maximos sic tribunicia potestate XXXIII imperator VIIII consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Antonini Augusti filius divi Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Nervae adnepos Germanicus Sarmaticus tribunicia potestate III sic imperator II consul II pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis quattuor quae appellantur veterana Gallica et Thracum Mauretanica et Vocontiorum et Apriana provincialis et cohortibus VIIII I Ulpia equitata Afrorum et I Augusta nnum et I Apamenorum et I Lusitanorum et I Flavia equitata Cilicum et II Thracum et I sic Ituraeorum et III Ituraeorum et II Thebeorum et sunt in Aegupto sic sub Pactumeio Magno praefecto quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis \n
EDCS-12100050.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, son of the deified Antoninus, brother of the deified Verus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 33rd year, Imperator for the 9th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Country, and to Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus, son of Antoninus Augustus, grandson of the deified Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Germanicus Sarmaticus, with tribunician power for the 3rd year, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 2nd time, Father of the Country. To the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the four alae called Veteran Gallica, Thracum Mauretanica, Vocontiorum, and Apriana provincialis, and in the nine cohorts: I Ulpia equitata Afrorum, I Augusta nnorum, I Apamenorum, I Lusitanorum, I Flavia equitata Cilicum, II Thracum, I Ituraeorum, III Ituraeorum, and II Thebeorum, and are in Egypt, under Pactumeius Magnus, prefect. Having completed twenty-five or more years of service, honorably discharged, to whom the Roman citizenship was granted, for those who did not have it, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or with those they would marry later, but only one wife each, before the 10th day before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Titus Flavius Claudianus and Lucius Aemilius Junius. Cohors II Thracum, commanded by Caecilius Maior, from the infantry: Plutioni, son of Tithoetes, from Coptos. Described and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near Minerva. [The imperial titles are repeated]. To the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the four alae called Veteran Gallica, Thracum Mauretanica, Vocontiorum, and Apriana provincialis, and in the nine cohorts: I Ulpia equitata Afrorum, I Augusta nnum, I Apamenorum, I Lusitanorum, I Flavia equitata Cilicum, II Thracum, I Ituraeorum, III Ituraeorum, and II Thebeorum, and are in Egypt, under Pactumeius Magnus, prefect. Having completed twenty-five or more years of service, honorably discharged, to whom the Roman citizenship was granted, for those who did not have it, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or with those they would marry later, but only one wife each.
EDCS-12100050.summary: This is a grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of several cavalry alae and infantry cohorts serving in Egypt, issued under the reigns of Marcus Aurelius and Commodus.

EDCS-12300228.text_edition: \n Imperator Titus Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XIIII pater patriae censor consul VII veteranis qui militaverunt in classe quae est in Aegypto emeritis stipendiis senis et vicenis pluribusve dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem VI Idus Septembres Tito Rubrio Aelio Nepote Marco Arrio Flacco consulibus ex remigibus Marco Papirio Marci filio Arsenoitae et Tapaiae Tryphonis filiae uxori eius et Carpinio filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio in basi Pompili regis ad aram gentis Iuliae Imperator Titus Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XIIII pater patriae censor consul VII veteranis qui militaverunt in classe quae est in Aegypto emeritis stipendiis senis et vicenis pluribusve dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem VI Idus Septembres Tito Rubrio Aelio Nepote Marco Arrio Flacco consulibus ex remigibus Marco Papirio Marci filio Arsenoitae et Tapaiae Tryphonis filiae uxori eius et Carpinio filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio Publi Atini Rufi Marci Stlacci Phileti Luci Pulli Sperati Quinti Muci Augustalis Luci Pulli Verecundi Luci Pulli Ianuari Titi Vibi Zosimi\n
EDCS-12300228.translation: The Emperor Titus Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 9th year, Imperator for the 14th time, Father of the Country, Censor, Consul for the 7th time. To the veterans who served in the fleet which is in Egypt, having completed twenty-six or more years of service, honorably discharged, to whom the Roman citizenship was granted, for them, their children, and descendants, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if any were unmarried, with those they would marry later, but only one wife each, before the 6th day before the Ides of September, in the consulship of Titus Rubrius Aelius Nepos and Marcus Arrius Flaccus. From the rowers: Marcus Papirius, son of Marcus, from Arsinoe, and Tapaia, daughter of Tryphon, his wife, and Carpinus, their son. Described and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, in the base of King Numa, near the altar of the Julian family. [Imperial titles repeated]. To the veterans who served in the fleet which is in Egypt, having completed twenty-six or more years of service, honorably discharged, to whom the Roman citizenship was granted, for them, their children, and descendants, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if any were unmarried, with those they would marry later, but only one wife each, before the 6th day before the Ides of September, in the consulship of Titus Rubrius Aelius Nepos and Marcus Arrius Flaccus. From the rowers: Marcus Papirius, son of Marcus, from Arsinoe, and Tapaia, daughter of Tryphon, his wife, and Carpinus, their son. Described and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, by Publius Atinius Rufus, Marcus Stlaccius Philetus, Lucius Pullius Speratus, Quintus Mucius Augustalis, Lucius Pullius Verecundus, Lucius Pullius Ianuarius, Titus Vibius Zosimus.
EDCS-12300228.summary: This inscription grants Roman citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of the fleet stationed in Egypt, issued by Emperor Vespasian.

EDCS-12300233.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300233.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Vespasian, Domitianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 2nd time, imperator for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, consul for the 9th time, designated consul for the 10th time, granted citizenship and the right of legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantrymen serving in three alae and seven cohorts named I Augusta, Apriana, Commagenorum, I Pannoniorum, I Hispanorum, I Flavia Cilicum, I and II Thebaeorum, and II and III Ituraeorum, stationed in Egypt under Lucius Laberius Maximus, who had completed twenty-five years of service or more, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants. He also granted the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they would later marry, provided each man married only one woman. This was enacted on the 5th day before the Ides of June, when Tettius Iulianus and Terentius Strabo Erucius Homullus were consuls. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitoline Hill, in the vicinity of the I Hispanorum cohort, commanded by Marcus Sabinius Fuscus, for the centurion Gaius Julius Saturninus, son of Gaius Julius, from Chios.
EDCS-12300233.summary: This inscription records an imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights to auxiliary soldiers serving in Egypt, issued by Emperor Domitian and details the record-keeping process.

EDCS-12300236.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300236.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Vespasian, Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 11th time, perpetual censor, consul for the 12th time, father of the fatherland, granted citizenship and the right of legal marriage to the sailors serving in Egypt under Gaius Septimius Vegetus and Claudius Clemens, prefect of the fleet, and also to those honorably discharged from the same fleet after completing twenty-six years of service or more, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants. He also granted the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they would later marry, provided each man married only one woman. This was enacted on the 13th day before the Kalends of March, when Gaius Secius Campanus and Servius Cornelius Dolabella Petronianus were consuls. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitoline Hill, behind the trophies of Germanicus near the temple of Fides Populi Romani, for Gaius Gemellus, son of Cronius, from Coptos.
EDCS-12300236.summary: This inscription details Emperor Domitian's grant of citizenship and marriage rights to sailors of the Egyptian fleet who had completed long service, with the record being fixed on the Capitoline Hill.

EDCS-12500245.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti domini nostri Sulpicius Serenus aram constituitII qui biduo secutus Agriophagos nequissimos quorum fere pars maior in pugna perit neque vulnera neque C--Raccepit praedamque totam cum camelis apstulit sic \xe1\xbd\x91\xcf\x80\xe1\xbd\xb2\xcf\x81 \xcf\x83\xcf\x89\xcf\x84\xce\xb7\xcf\x81\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xb1\xcf\x82 \xce\x91\xe1\xbd\x90\xcf\x84\xce\xbf\xce\xba\xcf\x81\xe1\xbd\xb1\xcf\x84\xce\xbf\xcf\x81\xce\xbf\xcf\x82 \xce\x9a\xce\xb1\xe1\xbd\xb7\xcf\x83\xce\xb1\xcf\x81\xce\xbf\xcf\x82 \xce\xa4\xcf\x81\xce\xb1\xce\xb9\xce\xb1\xce\xbd\xce\xbf\xe1\xbf\xa6 \xe1\xbc\x89\xce\xb4\xcf\x81\xce\xb9\xce\xb1\xce\xbd\xce\xbf\xe1\xbf\xa6 \xce\xa3\xce\xb5\xce\xb2\xce\xb1\xcf\x83\xcf\x84\xce\xbf\xe1\xbf\xa6 \xcf\x84\xce\xbf\xe1\xbf\xa6 \xce\xba\xcf\x85\xcf\x81\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xbf\xcf\x85 \xce\xa3\xce\xbf\xcf\x85\xce\xbb\xcf\x80\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xba\xce\xb9\xce\xbf\xcf\x82 \xcf\x85\xe1\xbc\xb1\xe1\xbd\xb8\xcf\x82 \xce\x93\xce\xbd\xce\xb1\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xbf\xcf\x85 \xce\x9a\xce\xbf\xcf\x85\xce\xb9\xcf\x81\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xbd\xce\xb1 \xce\xa3\xce\xb5\xcf\x81\xce\xb7\xce\xbd\xe1\xbd\xb8\xcf\x82 \xe1\xbc\x88\xce\xb3\xcf\x81\xce\xb9\xce\xbf\xcf\x86\xe1\xbd\xb1\xce\xb3\xce\xbf\xcf\x85\xcf\x82 \xce\xb4\xce\xb5\xce\xb9\xce\xbd\xce\xbf\xcf\x84\xe1\xbd\xb1\xcf\x84\xce\xbf\xcf\x85\xcf\x82 \xce\xbd\xce\xb9\xce\xba\xe1\xbd\xb5\xcf\x83\xce\xb1\xcf\x82 \xcf\x84\xe1\xbd\xb8\xce\xbd \xce\xb2\xcf\x89\xce\xbc\xe1\xbd\xb9\xce\xbd\n
EDCS-12500245.translation: For the safety of our lord Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Sulpicius Serenus erected this altar. Having pursued the most wicked Agriophagi for two days, the majority of whom perished in battle, [he] received no wounds, but carried off all the plunder with the camels. This altar was dedicated by Sulpicius Serenus, the devoted son of Sebasthus, to the emperor Trajan Hadrian, who is the provider and sustainer of the world, and to his son, the emperor Hadrian, in honor of the victory over the Agriophagi.
EDCS-12500245.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar by Sulpicius Serenus to honor Emperor Hadrian for a victory over the Agriophagi in Egypt, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-12500247.text_edition: \n Lucio Nonio Calpurnio Torquato Asprenate Tito Sextio Magio Laterano consulibus VI Nonas Iulias anno XIII Imperatoris Caesaris Domitiani Augusti Germanici mense Epiph die VIII Alexandriae ad Aegyptum Marcus Valerius Marci filius Pollia Quadratus veteranus dimmissus honesta missione ex legione X fretense testatus est se descriptum et recognitum fecisse ex tabula aenea quae est fixa in Caesareo magno escendentium scalas secundas sub porticum dexteriorem secus aedem Veneris marmoreae in pariete in qua scriptum est et id quod infra scriptum est Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XXVI censor perpetuus pater patriae dicit visum est mihi edicto significare universorum vestrorum u<t=I> veterani milites omnibus vectigalibus portitoribus sic publicis liberati immunes esse debeant ipsi coniuges liberique eorum parentes qui conubia eorum sument omni optumo iure cives Romani esse possint et omni immunitate liberati apsolutique sic sint et omnem immunitatem qui supra scripti sunt parentes liberique eorum idem iuris idem condicionis sint utique praedia domus tabernae invitos intemni qui veteranos s--onis veteranorum cum uxoribus et liberis supra scriptis in aere incisis aut si qui caelibes sint cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas qui militaverunt Hierosolymnis sic in legione X fretense dimmissorum honesta missione stipendis emeritis per Sextum Hermetidium Campanum legatum Augusti pro praetore V Kalendas Ianuarias Sexto Pompeio Collega Quinto Peducaeo Priscino consulibus qui militare coeperunt Publio Galerio Trachalo Tiberio Catio et Tito Flavio Cnaeo Aruleno consulibus ex permissu Marci Iuni Rufi praefecti Aegypti Lucio Nonio Calpurnio Torquato Asprenate Tito Sextio Magio Laterano consulibus Kalendis Iuliis anno XIII Imperatoris Caesaris Domitiani Augusti Germanici mense Epiph die VII ibi Marcus Valerius Marci filius Pollia Quadratus coram ac praesentibus eis qui signaturi erant testatus est iuratusque dixit per Iovem Optimum Maximum et Genium sacratissimi Imperatoris Caesaris Domitiani Augusti Germanici in militia sibi Lucium Valerium Valentem et Valeriam Heracliu<u=M> et Valeriam Artemin omnes tres supra scriptos natos esse eosque in aere incisos civitatem Romanam consecutos esse beneficio eiusdem optumi principis Caius Epidius Cai filius Pollia Bassus veteranus Lucius Petronius Luci filius Pollia Crispus veteranus Marcus Plotius Marci filius Pollia Fuscus veteranus Marcus Antonius Marci filius Pollia Celer veteranus Publius Audasius Publi filius Pollia Paullus veteranus Marcus Antonius Longus Pullia sic veteranus Lucius Petronius Niger veteranus Lucius Valerius Clemens veteranus Marcus Antonius Germanus veteranus \n
EDCS-12500247.translation: Under the consulship of Lucius Nonius Calpurnius Torquatus Asprenas and Titus Sextius Magius Lateranus, on the 6th day before the Nones of July, in the 13th year of Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, in the month of Epiph, on the 8th day, in Alexandria in Egypt, Marcus Valerius Quadratus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, a veteran honorably discharged from the Xth Legion Fretensis, testified that he had this recorded and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed in the great Caesareum, on the second ascending staircase, to the right portico, next to the marble temple of Venus, on which wall is inscribed what follows: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Vespasian, Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 8th time, imperator for the 26th time, perpetual censor, father of the fatherland, declares that it is my will to make known to all of you that veteran soldiers should be freed and exempt from all public taxes and tolls, and that they themselves, their spouses, and their children may be Roman citizens with the best right, and be completely free and released from all exemption, and that their parents, children, and descendants should have the same rights and conditions, and that the estates, houses, and shops of those who have served in the Xth Legion Fretensis and have been honorably discharged with stipends, along with their wives and children inscribed on bronze, or if they are single, with those they will marry afterwards, provided each man marries only one woman, should not be disturbed by those who disturb veterans. This was proclaimed by Sextus Hermetidius Campanus, legate of the emperor with proconsular authority, on the 5th day before the Kalends of January, under the consulship of Sextus Pompeius Collega and Quintus Peducaeus Priscinus, when they began their service under Publius Galerius Trachalus and Tiberius Catius Sacerdos and Titus Flavius Cn. Arulenus consulibus, by permission of Marcus Junius Rufus, prefect of Egypt, under Lucius Nonius Calpurnius Torquatus Asprenas and Titus Sextius Magius Lateranus consulibus, on the Kalends of July, in the 13th year of Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, in the month of Epiph, on the 7th day. There, Marcus Valerius Quadratus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, testified and swore by Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the most sacred Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus that Lucius Valerius Valens, Valeria Heracliu, and Valeria Artemin, all three mentioned above, were born to him in military service, and that by being inscribed on bronze they obtained Roman citizenship through the benefit of the same excellent emperor. Caius Epidius Bassus, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, veteran; Lucius Petronius Crispus, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, veteran; Marcus Plotius Fuscus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, veteran; Marcus Antonius Celer, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, veteran; Publius Audasius Paullus, son of Publius, of the Pollia tribe, veteran; Marcus Antonius Longus, of the Pullia tribe, veteran; Lucius Petronius Niger, veteran; Lucius Valerius Clemens, veteran; Marcus Antonius Germanus, veteran.
EDCS-12500247.summary: This lengthy inscription records a veteran's testimony about the granting of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Domitian to veterans of the Xth Legion Fretensis, along with a detailed list of accompanying officials and individuals.

EDCS-12700105.text_edition: \n Pacis aeternae propagatorem et publicae securitatis conservatorem dominum nostrum Galerium Valerium Maximianum Pium Felicem Invictum Augustum Aurelius Maximinus vir perfectissimus dux Aegypti et Thebaidos utrarumque Libbyarum devotus numini maiestatique eorum\n
EDCS-12700105.translation: To our lord, the propagator of eternal peace and preserver of public security, Galerius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Aurelius Maximinus, a most perfect man, duke of Egypt and Thebaid and of both Libyas, devoted to their divine majesty.
EDCS-12700105.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aurelius Maximinus, duke of Egypt, to Emperor Galerius Valerius Maximianus, praising him as a promoter of peace and security.

EDCS-12700106.text_edition: \n Iuventutis auctorem et pacis aeternae conservatorem dominum nostrum Flavium Valerium Constantinum nobilissimum Caesarem Aurelius Maximinus vir perfectissimus dux Aegypti et Thebaidos utrarumque Libbyarum devotus numini maiestatique eorum\n
EDCS-12700106.translation: To our lord, the promoter of youth and preserver of eternal peace, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, most noble Caesar, Aurelius Maximinus, a most perfect man, duke of Egypt and Thebaid and of both Libyas, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-12700106.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aurelius Maximinus, duke of Egypt, to Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, hailing him as the source of youth and preserver of peace.

EDCS-12700107.text_edition: \n nobilissimum Caesarem pontificem maximum tribunicia potestate X consulem III Aurelius Reginus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Thebaidos numini maiestatique eius semper dicatissimus\n
EDCS-12700107.translation: To the most noble Caesar, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 3rd time, Aurelius Reginus, a most perfect man, praeses of the province of Thebaid, always devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-12700107.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aurelius Reginus, praeses of Thebaid, to a noble Caesar (likely Constantine), praising his imperial titles.

EDCS-12700108.text_edition: \n Domino nostro fortissimo ac benignissimo Galerio Valerio Maximiano nobilissimo Caesari Valerius Euethius vir perfectissimus rationalis devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-12700108.translation: To our lord, the most brave and benevolent Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, Valerius Euethius, a most perfect man, rationalis, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-12700108.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Valerius Euethius, rationalis, to Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, acknowledging him as a brave and benevolent ruler.

EDCS-13302372.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name and possibly a title or affiliation, of Marcus Porcius Marcellus.

EDCS-15000157.text_edition: \n Antonius Silvanus eques alae I Thracum Mauretaniae strator praefecti turma Valeri testamentum fecit omnium bonorum meorum castrensium et domesticorum Marcus Antonius Satanus filius meus ex asse mihi heres esto ceteri ali omnes exheredes sunto cernitoque hereditatem meam in diebus C proximis ne sic ita creverit exheres esto tunc secundo grado sic Antonius Rlis frater meus mihi heres esto cernitoque hereditatem meam in diebus LX proximus sic qui sic do lego si mihi heres non erit |(denarios) argenteos septingentos quinquaginta procuratorem bonorum meorum castrensium ad bona mea colligenda et restituenda Antoniae Thermuthae matri heredis mei supra scripti facio Hieracem Behecis duplicarium alae eiusdem turma Aebuti ut et ipsa servet donec filius meus et heres suae tutellae sic fuerit et tunc ab ea recipiat qui sic do lego |(denarios) argenteos quinquaginta do lego Antoniae Thermuthae matri heredis mei supra scripti |(denarios) argenteos quingentos do lego praefecto meo |(denarios) argenteos quinquaginta Cronionem servom sic meum post mortem meam si omnia recte tractaverit et tradiderit heredi meo supra scripto vel procuratori tunc liberum volo esse vicesima<m=N>que pro eo ex bonis meis dari volo hoc testamento dolus malus <a=H>(besto) familia pequiniae sic quae testamenti faciendi causa emit Nemonium sic duplicarius turmae Mari libripende Marco Iulium sic Tiberinum sic sesquiplicario turmae Valeri antestatus est Turbinium signiferum turmae Proculi testamentum factum Alexandriae ad Aegyptum in castris Augustis hibernis legionis II Traianae fortis et alae Mauretaniae VI Kalendas Apriles Rufino et Quadrato consulibus Nemonius duplicarius turmae Mari signavi Turbinius eques signifer turmae Proculi Valerius Rufus eques signifer E turmae vis Maximus duplicarius usti si \n
EDCS-15000157.translation: Antonius Silvanus, an equestrian officer of the ala I Thracum Mauretaniae, strator to the prefect, from the turma of Valerius, made his will. My son, Marcus Antonius Satanus, shall be my sole heir of all my military and domestic property. All others are disinherited. He must accept my inheritance within the next 100 days, otherwise he is disinherited. Then, in the second degree, my brother Antonius Rlis shall be my heir. He must accept my inheritance within 60 days. If he is not my heir, I give and bequeath 750 silver denarii. I appoint Hierax, son of Behecis, a duplicarius of the same ala, turma Aebuti, as the procurator of my military property to collect and restore it to my aforementioned mother, Antonia Thermutha, the heir's mother, so that she may keep it until my son and heir reaches guardianship, and then she shall receive it from her. I give and bequeath 50 silver denarii. I give 500 silver denarii to Cronio, my slave, if he manages and delivers all my affairs correctly after my death to my aforementioned heir or procurator, then I wish him to be free, and I wish the twentieth part to be given for him from my property. This will is valid, free from malicious intent. The family and property acquired for the purpose of making this will. Nemonius, a duplicarius of the turma Mari, acted as the weigher (libripens), and Marcus Julius Tiberinus, a sesquiplicarius of the turma Valeri, was the witness. Turbinius, a standard-bearer of the turma Proculi, was the witness. The will was made in Alexandria in Egypt, in the Augustan winter camp of the II Traiana Fortis legion and the Mauretanian ala, on the 6th day before the Kalends of April, when Rufinus and Quadratus were consuls. Nemonius, duplicarius of the turma Mari, signed. Turbinius, equestrian, standard-bearer of the turma Proculi. Valerius Rufus, equestrian, standard-bearer of the turma E. Maximus, duplicarius, of the usti.
EDCS-15000157.summary: This is a detailed will made by Antonius Silvanus, an equestrian officer serving in Egypt, to his family and property, specifying heirs, bequests, and manumission of a slave.

EDCS-15800286.text_edition: \n Caio Bellicio Calpurnio Torquato Publio Salvio Iuliano consulibus III Nonas Novembres anno XII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii mense Athyr die VII Alexandriae ad Aegyptum descriptum et recognitum factum ex tabula albi professionum liberorum natorum quae tabula proposita erat in atrio magno in qua scriptum fuit id quod infra scriptum est Caio Bellicio Calpurnio Torquato Publio Salvio Iuliano consulibus anno XII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii Marco Petronio Honorato praefecto Aegypti profestiones sic liberorum acceptae citra causarum cognitionem tabula V et post alia pagina III XVIII Kalendas Octobres Tiberius Iulius Dioscurides HSS V filiam natam Iuliam Ammonun sic ex Iulia Ammonario XIII Kalendas Septembres quae proximae fuerunt civem Romanam exscripsi ad Kalendarium Caio Bellicio Calpurnio Torquato Publio Salvio Iuliano consulibus III Nonas Novembres anno XII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii mense Athyr die VII Alexandriae ad Aegyptum descriptum et recognitum factum ex tabula albi professionum liberorum natorum quae tabula proposita erat in atrio magno in qua scriptum fuit id quod infra scriptum est Caio Bellicio Calpurnio Torquato Publio Salvio Iuliano consulibus anno XII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii Marco Petronio Honorato praefecto Aegypti professiones liberorum acceptae citra causarum cognitionem tabula V et post alia pagina III XVIII Kalendas Octobres Tiberius Iulius Dioscurides HSS V filiam natam Iuliam Ammonum ex Iulia Ammonario XIII Kalendas Septembres quae proximae fuerunt civem Romanam exscripsi ad Kalendarium Cai Iuli Prisci Cai Iuli Sereni Titi Flavi Macedonis Marci Servili Clementis Cai Iuli Lecinniani sic Publi Iuli Eueichi sic Luci Petroni Celeris\n
EDCS-15800286.translation: During the consulship of Gaius Bellicius Calpurnius Torquatus and Publius Salvius Julianus, on the third day before the Nones of November, in the twelfth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Athyr, on the seventh day, at Alexandria in Egypt, this was transcribed and verified from the register of declared children born in wedlock, which register was displayed in the great atrium, on which was written that which follows: During the consulship of Gaius Bellicius Calpurnius Torquatus and Publius Salvius Julianus, in the twelfth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, under Marcus Petronius Honoratus, Prefect of Egypt, the declarations of children accepted without judicial inquiry were recorded on tablet V and subsequent pages, on the third page: on the eighteenth day before the Kalends of October, Tiberius Iulius Dioscurides, a soldier of the fifth cohort, declared his daughter Iulia Ammonun, born to Iulia Ammonaris on the thirteenth day before the Kalends of September, who were the most recent ones, as a Roman citizen. I have transcribed this for the registrar during the consulship of Gaius Bellicius Calpurnius Torquatus and Publius Salvius Julianus, on the third day before the Nones of November, in the twelfth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Athyr, on the seventh day, at Alexandria in Egypt, this was transcribed and verified from the register of declared children born in wedlock, which register was displayed in the great atrium, on which was written that which follows: During the consulship of Gaius Bellicius Calpurnius Torquatus and Publius Salvius Julianus, in the twelfth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, under Marcus Petronius Honoratus, Prefect of Egypt, the declarations of children accepted without judicial inquiry were recorded on tablet V and subsequent pages, on the third page: on the eighteenth day before the Kalends of October, Tiberius Iulius Dioscurides, a soldier of the fifth cohort, declared his daughter Iulia Ammonun, born to Iulia Ammonaris on the thirteenth day before the Kalends of September, who were the most recent ones, as a Roman citizen. I have transcribed this for the registrar Caius Iulius Priscus, Caius Iulius Serenus, Titus Flavius Macedo, Marcus Servilius Clemens, Caius Iulius Lecinnianus, Publius Iulius Eueichus, Lucius Petronius Celer.
EDCS-15800286.summary: This is an official record from Alexandria, Egypt, dated to 145 AD, detailing the declaration of a child's birth and citizenship status under Prefect Marcus Petronius Honoratus, during the consulship of Calpurnius Torquatus and Salvius Julianus.

EDCS-15800287.text_edition: \n Sempronia Gemella tutore auctore Caio Iulio Saturnino testata est eos qui signaturi erant se enixam esse ex incerto patre XII Kalendas Apriles quae proximae fuerunt natos masculinos geminos eosqe vocitari MMarcos Sempronios Spuri filios Sarapionem et Socrationem ideoque se has testationes interposuisse dixit quia lex Aelia Sentia et Papia Poppaea spurios spuriasve in albo profiteri vetat de ea re eodem exemplo bis tabulae scriptae sunt actum Alexandriae ad Aegyptum III Kalendas Maias Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio IIII Marco Aurelio Caesare II consulibus anno VIII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii mense Pachon die IIII Sempronia Gemella tutore auctore Caio Iulio Saturnino testata est eos qui signaturi erant si enixam esse ex incerto patre XII Kalendas Apreles sic quae proximae fuerunt natos masculinos geminos eosque vocetari sic MMMarcos Sempronios Spuri filios Sarapionem et Socrationem ideoque se has testationes interposuisse dixit quia lex Aelia Sentia et Papia Poppaea spurios spuriasve in albo profiteri vetat de ea re eodem exempla bis tabulae scriptae sunt actum Alexandriae ad Aegyptum III Kalendas Maias Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio IIII Marco Aurelio Caesare II consulibus anno VIII Imperatoris Caesare sic Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii mense Pachon die IIII Marci Vibi Pollionis Marci Octavi Sereni Luci Aemili Maximi Luci Caponi Saturnini Cai Aebuti Saturnini Cai Vibievii sic Crassi Marci Holconi Ampissi\n
EDCS-15800287.translation: Sempronia Gemella, with her guardian Gaius Iulius Saturninus assenting, declared that she had given birth to twins, sons of an unknown father, on the twelfth day before the Kalends of April, which were the most recent ones, and that they are to be named Marcus Sempronius, sons of Spurius, Sarapion and Socration. She therefore stated that she made these declarations because the laws Aelia Sentia and Papia Poppaea forbid the declaration of illegitimate children. Two tablets were written concerning this matter in the same manner. This was done at Alexandria in Egypt on the third day before the Kalends of May, during the reign of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius for the fourth time and Marcus Aurelius Caesar for the second time as consuls, in the eighth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Pachon, on the fourth day. Sempronia Gemella, with her guardian Gaius Iulius Saturninus assenting, declared that she had given birth to twins, sons of an unknown father, on the twelfth day before the Kalends of April, which were the most recent ones, and that they are to be named Marcus Sempronius, sons of Spurius, Sarapion and Socration. She therefore stated that she made these declarations because the laws Aelia Sentia and Papia Poppaea forbid the declaration of illegitimate children. Two tablets were written concerning this matter in the same manner. This was done at Alexandria in Egypt on the third day before the Kalends of May, during the reign of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius for the fourth time and Marcus Aurelius Caesar for the second time as consuls, in the eighth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Pachon, on the fourth day, under Marcus Vibius Pollio, Marcus Octavius Serenus, Lucius Aemilius Maximus, Lucius Caponius Saturninus, Caius Aebutius Saturninus, Caius Vibievii Crassus, Marcus Holconius Ampsis.
EDCS-15800287.summary: This is an official declaration from Alexandria, Egypt, dated to 155 AD, where Sempronia Gemella, with her guardian's consent, registers the birth of her twin sons, acknowledging their unknown father due to legal requirements concerning the registration of children under the laws Aelia Sentia and Papia Poppaea.

EDCS-15900041.text_edition: \n Arsute Luci filia hospitae suae idcirco hanc testationem interposuisse se dixit propter districtionem militum actum Philadelphiae hibernis cohortis II Thebaeorum VII Kalendas Ianuarias Sergio Octavio Laenate Pontiano Marco Antonio Rufino cons(ulibus) anno XVI Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti mense Choeac die XXX Epimachus Longini miles cohortis II Thebaeorum centuria Octavi Alexandri filiam sibi natam esse Longiniam VII Kalendas Ianuarias quae proxumae fuerunt ex Arsute Luci filia hospitae suae idcirco hanc testationem interposuisse se dixit propter districtionem militiae actum Philadelphiae hibernis cohortis II Thebaeorum VII Kalendas Ianuarias Sergio Octavio Laenate Pontiano Marco Antonio Rufino consulibus anno XVI Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti mense Choeac die XXX\n
EDCS-15900041.translation: Arsute, daughter of Lucius, states that she has made this declaration because of the troop assignment of soldiers. This was done at Philadelphia in the winter quarters of the II Thebaean Cohort on the seventh day before the Kalends of January, during the consulship of Sergius Octavius Laenas Pontianus and Marcus Antonius Rufus, in the sixteenth year of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, in the month of Choeac, on the thirtieth day. Epimachus, son of Longinus, a soldier of the II Thebaean Cohort, in the centuria of Octavius Alexander, declared that his daughter Longinia was born to him on the seventh day before the Kalends of January, which were the most recent ones, by Arsute, daughter of Lucius, his 'hospita' (a woman with whom he had a guest-friendship relationship). He therefore stated that he has made this declaration because of the troop assignment in military service. This was done at Philadelphia in the winter quarters of the II Thebaean Cohort on the seventh day before the Kalends of January, during the consulship of Sergius Octavius Laenas Pontianus and Marcus Antonius Rufus, in the sixteenth year of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, in the month of Choeac, on the thirtieth day.
EDCS-15900041.summary: This inscription, dated to 133 AD, records a soldier, Epimachus, declaring the birth of his daughter, Longinia, to Arsute, with whom he had a guest-friendship, due to military deployment requirements.

EDCS-15900140.text_edition: \n Caius Antonius Maximus armorum custos Luci Farsulei Marcus Arrius Antoninus turma Rufi Gaius Barga miles Luci Farsulei Caius Iulius Marcellus cornicularius VDI T Marsias Bammogalis SVXI Numerius Alexa filius Longi Marcus Lucretius Clemens Marcus Lucretius Clemens eques cohortis I Thracum turma Silvani testatus est eos qui signaturi erant iuravitque per Iovem Optimum Maximum et numina divorum Augustorum Geniumque Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti naturalem sibi filium in militia natum esse Serenum ex Octavia Tamusta VII Kalendas Maias nno XI Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti Ut possit post honestam missionem suam ad epicrisin suam adprobare filium suum naturalem esse actum castris hibernis cohortis I Thracum contra Apollonos polim magnam Thebaidis Kalendis Mais nno supra scripto\n
EDCS-15900140.translation: Gaius Antonius Maximus, armor bearer, Lucius Farsuleius, Marcus Arrius Antoninus of the troop of Rufus, Gaius Barga, soldier of Lucius Farsuleius, Gaius Iulius Marcellus, cornicularius (horn player/standard bearer), Marsias Bammogalis, Numerius, son of Alexa and Longus, Marcus Lucretius Clemens, Marcus Lucretius Clemens, an equestrian of the I Thracian Cohort, in the troop of Silvanus, has declared and sworn by Jupiter Optimus Maximus, and the divine spirits of the Augusti, and the Genius of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, that he has a natural son born to him in military service, Serenus, by Octavia Tamusta, on the seventh day before the Kalends of May, in the eleventh year of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, so that he may be able to present him for approval after his honorable discharge. This was done in the winter camp of the I Thracian Cohort, opposite Apollonos polis magna in the Thebaid, on the Kalends of May, in the year written above.
EDCS-15900140.summary: This inscription from 128 AD records a soldier, Marcus Lucretius Clemens of the I Thracian Cohort, declaring his natural son, Serenus, born to Octavia Tamusta, to ensure the child's recognition after his military service.

EDCS-15900141.text_edition: \n Marco Opellio Antonino Diadumeniano nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis Augusti nostri filio sub Iulio Basiliano praefecto Aegypti cohors III Cilicum equitata curante Furnio Diabone |(centurione) legionis II Traianae fortis\n
EDCS-15900141.translation: To Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, our Augustus, son, under Iulius Basilianus, Prefect of Egypt, the III Cohort of Cilicians cavalry, under the supervision of Furnius Diabo, centurion of the Legio II Traiana Fortis.
EDCS-15900141.summary: This is a dedication or honorific inscription from Egypt, dated around 217-218 AD, honoring Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, son of the emperor, and mentioning the III Cohort of Cilicians and a centurion.

EDCS-16000482.translation: --- Getelius, son of ---, born in Rome, sixteen years old.
EDCS-16000482.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly from Rome, dating to the Roman Imperial period, recording the name 'Getelius' and his age of sixteen.

EDCS-16200739.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio IIII Marco Aeaelio Caesare II consulibus XVI Kalendas Iunias anno VIII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii mense Pachon die XXII Alexandreae ad Aegyptum descriptum et recognitum ex tabula albi professionum quae proposita erat in atrio magno in qua scriptum fuit id quod infra scriptum est Lucio Lolliano Avito Tito Statilio Maximo consulibus anno VIII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii Lucio Valerio Proculo praefecto Aegypti professiones liberorum acceptae citra causarum cogni[tionem ------\n
EDCS-16200739.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius for the fourth time and Marcus Aelius Caesar for the second time, on the sixteenth day before the Kalends of June, in the eighth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Pachon, on the twenty-second day, at Alexandria in Egypt, this was transcribed and verified from the register of declared children, which was displayed in the great atrium, on which was written that which follows: During the consulship of Lucius Lollius Avitus and Titus Statilius Maximus, in the eighth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, under Lucius Valerius Proculus, Prefect of Egypt, the declarations of children accepted without judicial inquiry [were recorded] ---.
EDCS-16200739.summary: This official record from Alexandria, Egypt, dated to 155 AD, concerns the registration of declared children under Prefect Lucius Valerius Proculus during the consulship of Lollius Avitus and Statilius Maximus.

EDCS-16200740.text_edition: \n Marco Pontio Laeliano Aulo Iunio Pastore consulibus X Kalendas Decembres anno IIII Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Aureli Veri Augusti mense Athyr die XXV Alexandreae ad Aegyptum describtum sic et recognitum ex tabula professiones sic liberi nati sunt quae proposita erat in atrio magno in qua scriptum fuit id quod infra scriptum est Marco Pontio Laeliano Aulo Iunio Pastore consulibus anno III Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Aureli Veri Augusti Marco Annio Suriaco praefecto Aegypti professiones liberorum acceptae citra causarum cognitionem tabula IIII pagina V V Idus Augustas Marcus Lucretius Octavianus absens per HS CCCC Publium Cornelium Bassum filium natum Marcum Lucretium Numisianum ex Iulia Cuaiane XIIII Kalendas Augustas quae proximae fuerunt domicilio Iuliopoli Aegypti crescentes ad Kalendas\n
EDCS-16200740.translation: During the consulship of Marcus Pontius Laelianus and Aulus Iunius Pastor, on the tenth day before the Kalends of December, in the fourth year of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, in the month of Athyr, on the twenty-fifth day, at Alexandria in Egypt, this was transcribed and verified from the register of declared children, which was displayed in the great atrium, on which was written that which follows: During the consulship of Marcus Pontius Laelianus and Aulus Iunius Pastor, in the third year of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, under Marcus Annius Suriacus, Prefect of Egypt, the declarations of children accepted without judicial inquiry were recorded on tablet IIII, page V: on the fifth day before the Ides of August, Marcus Lucretius Octavianus, absent, through [a payment of] 400 sesterces, [declared] Publius Cornelius Bassus, his son named Marcus Lucretius Numisianus, born to Iulia Cuaiane on the fourteenth day before the Kalends of August, which were the most recent ones, residing in Iuliopolis, Egypt, to be raised according to the Kalends.
EDCS-16200740.summary: This official record from Alexandria, Egypt, dated to 165 AD, details the declaration of Marcus Lucretius Octavianus, who posthumously registered his son, Marcus Lucretius Numisianus, with a payment, under Prefect Marcus Annius Suriacus.

EDCS-16200741.text_edition: \n Contentus heredi meo SS sepeliri me volo et in corpus meum consumi denaria Augusta ducenta S H dolus malus abesto factum Alexandreae ad Aegyptum Nonis Octobribus Barbaro et Regulo consulibus anno XXI Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii mense Phaophi die X Safinnius Herminus miles classis Augustae Alexandrinae tutela Tauro\n
EDCS-16200741.translation: Contentus wishes to be buried by my heir and for my estate to be consumed with two hundred Augustan denarii. Let malice be absent. Done at Alexandria in Egypt on the Nones of October, during the consulship of Barbarus and Regulus, in the twenty-first year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Phaophi, on the tenth day. Safinius Herminus, soldier of the Augustan fleet of Alexandria, under the guardianship of Tauros.
EDCS-16200741.summary: This is a tomb inscription from Alexandria, Egypt, dated to 158 AD, where a soldier of the Augustan fleet, Safinius Herminus, requests to be buried with his inheritance and specifies burial expenses, under the consulship of Barbarus and Regulus.

EDCS-16200743.text_edition: \n Lucio Lolliano Avito Tito Statilio Maximo consulibus Idibus Octobribus anno VIII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadrianiani Antonini Augusti Pii mense Phaophi die XVIII Alexandreae ad Aegyptum descriptum et recognitum ex tabula albi professionum quibus liberi nati sunt quae proposita erat in atrio magno in qua scriptum fuit id quod infra scriptum est Lucio Lolliano Avito Tito Statilio Maximo consulibus anno VII Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Piiia Lucio Valerio Proculo praefecto Aegypti professiones liberorum acceptae citra causarum cognitionem tabula II et post alia pagina II item anno VIII Imperatoris Antoninini domini nostri isdem consulibus item pagina III Idibus Septembribus Marcus Valerius Turbo filium natum SSBI Marcum Valerium Maximum ex Antonia Casullute XV Kalendas Septembres quae proximae fuerunt crescentes ad Kalendas\n
EDCS-16200743.translation: During the consulship of Lucius Lollius Avitus and Titus Statilius Maximus, on the Ides of October, in the eighth year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Phaophi, on the eighteenth day, at Alexandria in Egypt, this was transcribed and verified from the register of declared children, from which children were born, which was displayed in the great atrium, on which was written that which follows: During the consulship of Lucius Lollius Avitus and Titus Statilius Maximus, in the seventh year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, under Lucius Valerius Proclus, Prefect of Egypt, the declarations of children accepted without judicial inquiry were recorded on tablet II and subsequent pages, on the second page: also in the eighth year of our lord Emperor Antoninus, under the same consuls, also on the third page, on the Ides of September, Marcus Valerius Turbo declared his son Marcus Valerius Maximus, born to Antonia Casulluta on the fifteenth day before the Kalends of September, which were the most recent ones, to be raised according to the Kalends.
EDCS-16200743.summary: This official record from Alexandria, Egypt, dated to 155 AD, details the declaration of Marcus Valerius Turbo's son, Marcus Valerius Maximus, born to Antonia Casulluta, under Prefect Lucius Valerius Proculus.

EDCS-16200935.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Quintianus candidatus legionis II Traianae fortis cohortis VIIII hastatus posterior militavit nnos XIIII vixit nnos XXX Cilix Aurelius Demetrius centuria primi pili dispensator tribuni heres posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-16200935.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelius Quintianus, candidate of the Second Trajanic Legion, served in the Ninth Cohort as a junior soldier for 14 years, lived for 30 years. Cilix, Aurelius Demetrius, chief decurion and quartermaster, erected this for him as a well-deserving man, his heir.
EDCS-16200935.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aurelius Quintianus, a soldier in the Roman army, erected by his heir Aurelius Demetrius.
